introduction.title: Vietnamese Negation
content: Vietnamese negation is simple: place a negation word directly before the verb or adjective. There are no helper verbs like English 'do/does/did'.
Different negators carry different meanings: 'không' is general 'not', 'chưa' is 'not yet' (still pending), and 'đừng' is used to tell someone not to do something.
To negate identity (X is not Y), use 'không phải' before the noun — bare 'không' cannot negate nouns directly.

sections.title: General Negation
sections.description: How to say 'not' for verbs, adjectives, and identity

notes: The default negator — placed before verbs and adjectives.
Used alone, 'không' also means 'no' as an answer.
Cannot directly negate nouns — use 'không phải' instead.
Example: "I am not a doctor: Tôi ~~không~~ __không phải là__ bác sĩ" (use không phải là for identity/nouns).
Used before nouns to negate identity: 'không phải là bác sĩ' (is not a doctor).
Literally 'không' (not) + 'phải' (correct) — 'it is not the case that...'.
Usually followed by 'là' (to be): không phải là ...
Example: "This is not beautiful: Đây ~~không phải~~ __không__ đẹp" (use plain không for adjectives).
Stronger and more emphatic than 'không' — common in colloquial speech.
Often combined with 'cả' or 'gì cả' for 'not...at all'.
Slightly more dramatic or complaining tone than plain 'không'.
Colloquial variant of 'chẳng' — very common in everyday speech.
Same emphatic meaning but even more informal.

examples.vietnamese: Cô ấy không phải là giáo viên

examples.english: I don't know anything at all

sections.title: Not Yet
sections.description: Indicating something hasn't happened — but might still happen

notes: Implies the action is still pending or expected — different from final 'không'.
Question pattern: đã...chưa? (have you...yet?).
As a one-word answer, 'Chưa' means 'not yet'.
I don't like coffee: Tôi ~~chưa~~ __không__ thích cà phê (chưa is for pending actions, not states like liking).

sections.title: Negative Commands
sections.description: Telling someone not to do something

notes: The standard way to say 'don't' in commands and requests.
Softer than English imperatives — more like 'please don't'.
Often paired with 'nhé' at the end for politeness.
Example: "Don't do that!: ~~Không~~ __Đừng__ làm!" (use đừng for commands, not bare không).
More formal or literary than 'đừng' — common in proverbs and warnings.
Carries a stronger, more cautionary tone.
Less common in everyday casual speech.

sections.title: Negative Compounds
sections.description: Never, nobody, nothing, nowhere — formed with 'không' + question word

notes: Literally 'not' + 'ever' — placed before the verb.
Literally 'not' + 'who' — used as subject or object.
Literally 'not' + 'what'.
Literally 'not' + 'where'.
As a standalone response, means 'no way!' or 'not at all!' (dismissive reassurance).
